en.extract:
The afep pigeon (Columba unicincta), also known as the African woodpigeon or grey woodpigeon, is a member of the family Columbidae, native to the African tropical rainforest from Sierra Leone east to Uganda.


Taxonomy
The afep pigeon was described by the American ornithologist John Cassin in 1860 from a specimen collected in West Africa from the Ogooué River, Gabon. He coined the binomial name Columba unicincta. The specific epithet combines the Latin uni- "one-" and cinctus "banded". The English name "afep" is the word for a pigeon in the Bulu language of Cameroon. The species is monotypic.
Its genetic relationship to other species in the genus has not yet been tested; morphological traits suggest it belongs to the woodpigeon group comprising the common wood pigeon C. palumbus, Bolle's pigeon C. bollii, and Trocaz pigeon C. trocaz.


Description
The afep pigeon is 35–36 cm (13.8–14.2 in) length and weighs between 356–490 g (12.6–17.3 oz). The sexes are similar. It has a pale grey hind neck, pinkish-grey body, and dark grey wings; the tail has a broad white bar and a blackish tip. The throat and belly are white. The eyes and orbital rings are red.
This pigeon has a loud call. It sounds like "doo doo doo" or "whu whu whu whu-WHU".


Distribution
Afep pigeons are found in the African tropical rainforest, on either side of the Dahomey Gap. It is primarily a lowland species, but occurs locally up to 1,600 m altitude in Cameroon and Uganda.


Behaviour


Diet
Afep pigeons mainly feed on fruit, berries, grain and seeds. It has also been known to eat termites.


Reproduction
They mainly breed in the second half of the dry season. The female lays a single white egg. The egg is incubated for between 14 and 18 days, and both parents help raise the chick, which is fed with crop milk after hatching. A few days later, it will start eating small pieces of solid food. The chick fledges 20 to 25 days after they hatch.


Conservation
They are classified by the IUCN as of least concern. Their population is stable and unfragmented, though the exact number of birds is unknown. It is scarce to rare across much of its range, but locally common in the centre of its range in southern Cameroon, Gabon, and northwestern Angola.


References


External links
Xeno-canto: Audio recordings of the Afep pigeon
Photos, videos and observations at Cornell Lab of Ornithologys Birds of the World

The '''afep pigeon''' ('''''Columba unicincta'''''), also known as the '''African woodpigeon''' or '''grey woodpigeon''', is a member of the family [[Columbidae]], native to the [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|African tropical rainforest]] from [[Sierra Leone]] east to [[Uganda]].

==Taxonomy==
The afep pigeon was [[species description|described]] by the American ornithologist [[John Cassin]] in 1860 from a specimen collected in West Africa from the [[Ogooué River]], Gabon. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Columba unicincta''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Cassin | first=John | author-link=John Cassin | year=1860 | title=Catalogue of birds collected on the rivers Camma and Ogobai, West Africa, by Mr. P.B. Duchaillu in 1858, with notes and descriptions of new species | journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia | volume=11  | pages=30–144 [143] | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/1653081 }}</ref> The [[specific epithet]] combines the [[Latin]] ''uni-'' "one-" and ''cinctus'' "banded".<ref name=hbwkey>{{cite web | last=Jobling | first=J.A. | year=2018 | title= Key to Scientific Names in Ornithology | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=https://www.hbw.com/dictionary/definition/unicincta-unicinctus | access-date=16 February 2019 }}</ref> The English name "afep" is the word for a pigeon in the [[Bulu language]] of Cameroon.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Bates | first=George L. | year=1904 | title=Handbook of Bulu: Containing a Grammatical Sketch, Folk-tales for Reading and a Vocabulary | publisher=Richard Clay and Sons | page=114 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2019 | title=Pigeons | work=World Bird List Version 9.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/pigeons/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=16 February 2019 }}</ref>

Its genetic relationship to other species in the genus has not yet been tested; morphological traits suggest it belongs to the woodpigeon group comprising the [[common wood pigeon]] ''C. palumbus'', [[Bolle's pigeon]] ''C. bollii'', and [[Trocaz pigeon]] ''C. trocaz''.<ref name="HBW">{{cite book | last=Hoyo | first=Josep del | last2=Elliott | first2=Andrew | last3=Sargatal | first3=Jordi | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |volume=4 | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=1992 | isbn=84-87334-22-9 | page=116}}</ref>

== Description ==
The afep pigeon is {{cvt|35|–|36|cm|sigfig=3}} length and weighs between {{cvt|356|–|490|g}}.<ref name="HBW"/> The sexes are similar. It has a pale grey hind neck, pinkish-grey body, and dark grey wings; the tail has a broad white bar and a blackish tip. The throat and belly are white. The eyes and orbital rings are red.<ref name="HBW"/>

This pigeon has a loud call. It sounds like "doo doo doo" or "whu whu whu whu-WHU".<ref name="oiseaux-birds.com">{{Cite web|url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-speckled-pigeon.html|title=Speckled Pigeon}}</ref>

==Distribution==
Afep pigeons are found in the [[Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests|African tropical rainforest]], on either side of the [[Dahomey Gap]]. It is primarily a lowland species, but occurs locally up to 1,600 m altitude in [[Cameroon]] and [[Uganda]].<ref name="HBW"/>

== Behaviour ==
=== Diet ===
Afep pigeons mainly feed on fruit, berries, grain and seeds.<ref name="HBW"/><ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-speckled-pigeon.html|title=Speckled Pigeon|website=www.oiseaux-birds.com|access-date=2018-10-24}}</ref> It has also been known to eat [[termite]]s.<ref name="HBW"/>

=== Reproduction ===
They mainly breed in the second half of the dry season. The female lays a single white egg.<ref name="HBW"/> The egg is incubated for between 14 and 18 days, and both parents help raise the chick, which is fed with [[crop milk]] after hatching. A few days later, it will start eating small pieces of solid food. The chick fledges 20 to 25 days after they hatch.<ref name="oiseaux-birds.com"/>

== Conservation ==
They are classified by the IUCN as of least concern. Their population is stable and unfragmented, though the exact number of birds is unknown.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> It is scarce to rare across much of its range, but locally common in the centre of its range in southern [[Cameroon]], [[Gabon]], and northwestern [[Angola]].<ref name="HBW"/>
